Love it — your feature list is *exactly* the kind that benefits from a clean **component system + scalable architecture** so you can keep adding (sources, comparisons, tides, pollen, AQI, more charts) without the UI turning into a one-off per screen.

Below is a full redesign direction (flow, screens, SwiftUI view structure, design system, and refactor approach), **plus a first set of updated visual concepts** I generated for your tabs + Locations + Settings.

***

## Visual concepts (first pass mockups)

These are **concept comps** meant to match SwiftUI patterns (cards, pills, sheets, tab bar). They also show your “sparkline pill → modal chart” idea and “tap any card → detail modal” pattern.

**Today** (sparkline pills + hero observation card + hourly strip + tappable cards)  
![01\_today](data:image/png;base64,)

**Daily** (7‑day list rows with mini sparklines)  
![02\_daily](data:image/png;base64,)

**Map** (full map + bottom sheet layer pills)  
![03\_map](data:image/png;base64,)

**Station** (grid metrics + sparklines + “Compare sources” callout)  
![04\_station](data:image/png;base64,)

**Locations** (search + favorites + current location card)  
![05\_locations](data:image/png;base64,)

**Settings** (more prominent source + future data toggles)  
![06\_settings](data:image/png;base64,)

If you upload screenshots, I’ll iterate these to match your current data density, wording, and the exact “station metrics” you display.

***

## 1) App flow redesign (information architecture)

You already have the right top-level structure. The key redesign move is: **keep the 4 tabs stable**, and make everything else a **consistent “global overlay” pattern** (sheet / fullScreenCover / navigation destination).

### Tabs (top-level, persistent)

Apple’s HIG guidance is that tab bars are best for navigating **top-level sections**, keeping them visible, and avoiding extra/overflow tabs.   
So we keep: [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d1-f43c58d207335ce4a507cacfed5d6bd1/views/original/01_today.png)

*   **Today** (current obs + today forecast + “cards” for sun/alerts/etc.)
*   **Daily** (multi-day forecast + trends)
*   **Map** (radar + layers + time scrubber later)
*   **Station** (your backyard station metrics + charts)

### Global destinations (not tabs)

*   **Locations** (search + favorites + select active location)
*   **Settings**
*   **Compare Sources** (cross-cutting experience)
*   **Card Detail** / **Chart Detail** (modal detail for *anything*)

This matches HIG layout principles: clear hierarchy, grouping related items, and leaving secondary detail for another view. [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d12-ade2b53e90664b177b226c9a14a85fac/views/original/02_daily.png)

***

## 2) Core interaction model: “Everything is a card; everything can expand”

You explicitly want:

*   sparkline pill → tap → modal bigger chart + details
*   extend tap-to-expand to **any card**
*   keep the app extensible

✅ The trick is to standardize interaction around one type:

### **Card → Detail Sheet**

Every card is tappable. On tap:

*   present a **sheet** (or `NavigationStack` push on iPad)
*   show a **DetailView** that can include:
    *   a bigger chart
    *   metadata
    *   compare provider toggle
    *   share/export (future)

This keeps your home screens lean and lets you add future metrics without redesigning layout.

### Sparkline pills are just “small cards”

Sparkline pills become a specialized card variant:

*   compact
*   quick glance
*   always opens a chart detail sheet

***

## 3) Design system (SwiftUI-friendly, reusable)

This is the “foundation layer” that makes future features cheap.

### Color & materials

*   Use system materials / semantic colors so dark mode “just works”
*   Card backgrounds: `ultraThinMaterial` / custom surface color
*   Accents: one consistent `.tint` (e.g., sun-yellow / cyan)

HIG emphasizes hierarchy and clear separation of controls vs content; materials help make controls float above content. [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d12-ade2b53e90664b177b226c9a14a85fac/views/original/02_daily.png)

### Spacing & layout tokens

Define constants once:

*   `Spacing.xs/s/m/l`
*   `Radius.card`, `Radius.pill`
*   `Shadow.elevation1/2`

### Typography

*   Title: `.title.bold()`
*   Section headers: `.headline`
*   Metrics: `.system(.largeTitle, design: .rounded).weight(.semibold)` (optional)

### SF Symbols

Pick a consistent set for:

*   tabs
*   metrics (wind, pressure, humidity, UV, etc.)
*   layers

***

## 4) Screen-by-screen structure (what goes where)

### **Today**

Primary goal: “At a glance + fast drill-down”

*   Top nav: Location + “Compare” chip + “Locations” button
*   Row of **sparkline pills**: Temp / Wind / Pressure (and more later)
*   Hero observation card
*   Hourly strip card
*   Secondary cards (sun, alerts, air quality, pollen later)

### **Daily**

Primary goal: “Forecast list + trend hints”

*   Tomorrow hero row
*   7–14 day list rows with mini sparkline
*   (Future) tap a day → detail sheet with hourly breakdown + precip chart

### **Map**

Primary goal: “Map first; controls second”

*   Full-screen map
*   Bottom sheet: layers (Radar/Temp/Wind/Clouds/AQI)
*   Later: time scrubber + playback controls

### **Station**

Primary goal: “Your yard as a data dashboard”

*   Station header with status + last update
*   Grid of metric tiles (each tile = card = opens detail)
*   Optional: “Compare sources” callout card

### **Locations**

Primary goal: “fast switching”

*   search bar
*   favorites list
*   current pinned “Home (Station)”

### **Settings**

Primary goal: “make source more prominent”

*   Weather Source card at top (prominent)
*   Compare sources entry point
*   toggles for optional datasets (AQI, pollen, tides)

***

## 5) Charts & sparklines (Swift Charts approach)

### Why Swift Charts

Swift Charts is built for SwiftUI and provides marks/scales/axes with minimal code, plus customization via chart modifiers. [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d8-0ac33e83f8585865f025b770cf5391d7/views/original/03_map.png)

### Sparkline design

*   No axes
*   No grid
*   LineMark (or AreaMark for precip)
*   Consistent color mapping per metric type

### Detail chart

*   Show axes + labels
*   Add interactivity later:
    *   `chartOverlay` + `ChartProxy` to read values at gesture locations is the common pattern for making charts interactive. [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d6-4f5270bdc7d19eb92da4b61b26dd43c6/views/original/04_station.png), [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d3-d8ddc15dbcf425e6164dc8701fa91d8e/views/original/05_locations.png)

If you want inspiration for different chart styles and preview/detail patterns, this examples repo is handy. [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d5-8455d657385b16546e8a57140b6aca55/views/original/06_settings.png)

***

## 6) Extensible architecture (so adding tides/pollen/AQI isn’t painful)

The UI will scale if the data layer is modular.

### Provider model

Define protocols like:

*   `WeatherProvider` (forecast/observation)
*   `StationProvider` (your yard)
*   `AirQualityProvider`, `PollenProvider`, `TidesProvider`

Then **compose** capabilities based on what’s enabled.

### Why this matters

When you add “compare sources”, you don’t want `if provider == ...` sprinkled through views. You want:

*   `ActiveProvider`
*   `CompareProviderA/B`
*   identical `WeatherData` output model

### State management option

You can do this in MVVM (`@Observable` view models) or go more modular with TCA if you prefer explicit state composition and testability. TCA is explicitly designed for composable features, shared state, and side effects.   
If you want to keep it simpler, MVVM aligns naturally with SwiftUI’s data flow, with view models bridging model and view. [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/tab-bars/) [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/layout)

***

## 7) SwiftUI view structures (how to refactor cleanly)

Here’s the key pattern that unlocks “tap any card → modal detail” **without duplicating sheets everywhere**:

### A single “Sheet Router”

*   You store `@State var activeSheet: SheetRoute?`
*   Every card sets `activeSheet = .metric(.temperature)` etc.
*   One `.sheet(item:)` at the root presents the right detail view

Example structure (conceptual):

```swift
enum SheetRoute: Identifiable {
  case metric(MetricKind)
  case forecastDay(Date)
  case compareSources
  case settings
  case locationPicker

  var id: String { /* stable ids */ }
}

struct RootView: View {
  @State private var sheet: SheetRoute?

  var body: some View {
    TabView {
      TodayView(openSheet: { sheet = $0 })
      DailyView(openSheet: { sheet = $0 })
      MapView(openSheet: { sheet = $0 })
      StationView(openSheet: { sheet = $0 })
    }
    .sheet(item: $sheet) { route in
      SheetHost(route: route)
    }
  }
}
```

Now:

*   sparkline pill uses `openSheet(.metric(.pressure))`
*   station tile uses `openSheet(.metric(.windGust))`
*   settings button uses `openSheet(.settings)`

**One pattern. Infinite extensibility.**

***

## 8) “Compare sources” UX (make it powerful but not cluttered)

Two good entry points:

1.  A **Compare** chip in Today nav bar (like the mockup)
2.  A **Compare Sources** card in Station + Settings

Comparison UI modes:

*   **Overlay mode**: show Provider A vs B line charts on same axes
*   **Delta mode**: show “A – B” difference as a chart
*   **Table mode**: small differences summary for key metrics

All of these use the same `WeatherData` model output, so providers are swappable.

***

*   match your actual data density and card list
*   align typography and spacing to your content
*   redesign the station tiles exactly to your sensors/metrics

*   produce a **revised set of mockups** that mirror your real content
*   outline a **component checklist** (Card, SparklinePill, MetricTile, SheetRouter, ProviderPicker, CompareView)
*   and give you a **SwiftUI refactor plan** that you can implement incrementally without rewriting everything

(Go ahead and upload those two screenshots whenever you’re ready.)
